speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I've been feeling pretty unwell over the past few days.

speaker: doctor
text: Hello. Based on your chart and today's assessment, your primary diagnosis is Essential Hypertension, confirmed as of December 17th.

speaker: patient
text: Hypertension? I’ve had high blood pressure before, but lately I’ve noticed swelling in my ankles and calves—especially by evening.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, that lower limb edema started around December 17th—and it’s been getting worse.

speaker: doctor
text: I just checked your temperature—it’s elevated at 38.1 °C, also as of today.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is 127 mmHg—within target range for now, but we’ll monitor closely given the other symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also been feeling dizzy when standing up quickly, and my heart feels like it’s racing sometimes.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me check your pulse—yes, your heart rate is 115 bpm right now. That’s tachycardic, especially with fever and edema.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m prescribing Atorvastatin 40mg to be taken once daily at night, starting tonight, to support cardiovascular risk reduction.

speaker: doctor
text: Again, your heart rate is 115 bpm—this remains elevated and concerns me in the context of your fever and edema.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been checking my blood sugar at home too—I’m diabetic, though it’s usually well-controlled with diet.

speaker: doctor
text: Thank you for sharing that. Let’s run a point-of-care glucose test and assess whether this acute illness is affecting your glycemic control.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate has improved to 95 bpm—much better than the 115 we saw earlier. That suggests some response to hydration and rest, but we’re not out of the woods yet.

speaker: patient
text: My temperature just spiked again—I took it a few minutes ago and it’s 39.1 °C.

speaker: doctor
text: That confirms the worsening fever—we’ll need to investigate possible infection or inflammatory cause, especially with persistent edema and tachycardia.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been short of breath walking to the bathroom, and my legs feel heavy and tight.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your diabetes, rising temperature, and hemodynamic changes, we need tighter glycemic control while you’re acutely ill.

speaker: patient
text: My home glucometer readings have been hovering between 180–240 mg/dL the last two days.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll transition from oral agents to basal insulin temporarily. I’m going to start you on Insulin glargine 16 units once nightly.

speaker: doctor
text: And just to confirm—we’re continuing to monitor your blood pressure closely; that reading of 127 mmHg from earlier remains stable.
